stem: You compare a gate against a hand-written decomposition with `Operator(a) == Operator(b)` and get False, even though the two circuits are functionally the same unitary. Why can this happen, and what check should you use instead?

options.text: `==` compares Python object identity, not the matrices; compare the circuits with `np.array_equal` instead
A False from `==` proves the decomposition is wrong; there is no other reason two equal unitaries would compare unequal
They can differ by a global phase; `==` demands exact matrix equality, whereas `Operator.equiv` treats operators as equal up to a global phase — use `.equiv()`
`==` fails because the two circuits list their qubits in different orders; reorder the qubits and `==` will pass

explanation.correct: Two circuits implementing the same operation can produce unitaries that differ by an overall phase factor e^{iθ} (e.g. Z vs RZ(π) = −i·Z). `Operator.__eq__` requires the matrices to match exactly, so it returns False; `Operator.equiv` returns True when they match up to a global phase, which is the physically meaningful comparison.

distractors.D: Qubit-order differences are a separate issue and would produce a genuinely different matrix, not a global-phase discrepancy. Reordering does not address the phase, and `.equiv()` is the right tool here.
distractors.A: `Operator.__eq__` does compare the underlying matrices (numerically), not object identity, so this diagnosis is wrong.
distractors.B: A functionally-correct decomposition can still differ by a global phase, so a False from `==` does NOT by itself mean the decomposition is wrong — that is exactly why `.equiv()` exists.
